title: Physics Test 7 #PT007 (Motion under Gravity & Physical Properties of Materials)

questions.text: Satellite is kept moving in its orbit around the Earth; it is due to
options: Gravitational force or lack of it
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) Centripetal force

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 8 — Gravitation | Topic: Orbital motion; centripetal force; satellite dynamics

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — Centripetal vs centrifugal force in satellite motion is one of the most tested concepts in DRDO CEPTAM, UPSC IAS, and NDA exams.

---

A satellite in circular orbit requires a force directed toward the centre of the Earth to keep it moving in a curved path. This inward-directed force is called centripetal force.

For a satellite of mass m orbiting at radius r with speed v:

\( F_{centripetal} = \frac{mv^2}{r} \)

This centripetal force is provided entirely by Earth's gravitational attraction:

\( F_{gravity} = \frac{GMm}{r^2} \)

Setting them equal gives the orbital velocity:

\( v = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{r}} \)

---

(A) Centrifugal force — INCORRECT:
Centrifugal force is a pseudo-force that appears only in the rotating (non-inertial) reference frame of the satellite itself. In the inertial (ground) frame, no centrifugal force exists. The satellite is kept in orbit by centripetal force (gravity), not centrifugal force.

(B) Centripetal force — CORRECT:
Earth's gravity acts as the centripetal force, continuously pulling the satellite inward (toward Earth's centre), keeping it in circular orbit rather than flying off in a straight line (as Newton's First Law would dictate without a force).

(C) Gravitational force or lack of it — INCORRECT:
Gravitational force IS the centripetal force here — so this is partially right but misleadingly phrased. 'Lack of gravity' is wrong — gravity is very much present and is what keeps the satellite in orbit. The phrasing 'or lack of it' makes this option incorrect.

(D) Some other forces — INCORRECT:
No 'other forces' are involved. Only gravity (acting as centripetal force) keeps a satellite in orbit. There is no thrust, no magnetic force, no air drag (in ideal orbit).

🔬 DRDO Relevance: India's reconnaissance satellites (RISAT-2B) and communication satellites (GSAT series, used for military communications) are maintained in orbit purely by this centripetal gravitational force. DRDO collaborates with ISRO on satellite-based surveillance systems.

questions.text: The known forces of nature can be divided into four classes, viz., gravity, electromagnetism, weak nuclear force and strong nuclear force. With reference to them which one of the following statements is NOT correct?
options: Gravity is the strongest of the four
Electromagnetism acts only on particles with an electric charge
Strong nuclear force holds protons and neutrons inside the nucleus of an atom
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) Gravity is the strongest of the four

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion; Chapter 8 — Gravitation | Topic: Fundamental forces of nature; their relative strengths

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — The four fundamental forces and their properties is a standard IAS (Pre) and DRDO CEPTAM question. Tests conceptual depth in modern physics.

---

The four fundamental forces of nature in order of DECREASING strength:

\( F_{strong} > F_{electromagnetic} > F_{weak} > F_{gravity} \)

Relative strengths (approximate):
- Strong nuclear force: \( 1 \)
- Electromagnetic force: \( 10^{-2} \)
- Weak nuclear force: \( 10^{-13} \)
- Gravitational force: \( 10^{-38} \)

Gravity is actually the WEAKEST of the four fundamental forces — not the strongest.

---

(A) Gravity is the strongest of the four — INCORRECT (This IS the wrong statement, so this is the answer):
Gravity is the WEAKEST fundamental force. It is approximately \( 10^{38} \) times weaker than the strong nuclear force. Despite governing planetary motion, galaxies, and the universe at large scales, gravity is negligibly weak at the subatomic level.

(B) Electromagnetism acts only on particles with electric charge — CORRECT statement:
Electromagnetic force acts between electrically charged particles (protons, electrons). Neutral particles like neutrons do not experience electromagnetic force directly. This statement is TRUE.

(C) Weak nuclear force causes radioactivity — CORRECT statement:
The weak nuclear force is responsible for beta decay (a type of radioactive decay) where a neutron converts to a proton, emitting an electron (beta particle) and an antineutrino. This statement is TRUE.

(D) Strong nuclear force holds protons and neutrons inside the nucleus — CORRECT statement:
The strong nuclear force (also called strong interaction) binds quarks together to form protons and neutrons, and also holds protons and neutrons together inside the nucleus, overcoming the electromagnetic repulsion between protons. This statement is TRUE.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Understanding fundamental forces is essential for DRDO's nuclear weapons program (strong nuclear force → fission/fusion), electronic warfare systems (electromagnetic force), and the development of radiation detectors (weak force → radioactive decay) at BARC-DRDO collaborative facilities.

questions.text: If a person sitting in a lift, then when will he feel that his weight gets increased?
options: When the elevator going upward expeditiously
When the elevator going down expeditiously
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) When the elevator going upward expeditiously

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Apparent weight in a lift; Newton's Second Law application

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — Apparent weight in a lift is one of the most frequently asked numerical and conceptual questions in DRDO CEPTAM, IAS (Pre), NDA, and all engineering entrance exams.

---

Apparent weight (Normal force N) in a lift:

When lift accelerates UPWARD with acceleration a:
\( N = m(g + a) \)
Apparent weight INCREASES — person feels heavier.

When lift accelerates DOWNWARD with acceleration a:
\( N = m(g - a) \)
Apparent weight DECREASES — person feels lighter.

When in FREE FALL (cable snaps), a = g:
\( N = m(g - g) = 0 \)
Apparent weight = ZERO — complete weightlessness.

When moving at CONSTANT VELOCITY (up or down), a = 0:
\( N = mg \)
Apparent weight = SAME as normal weight.

---

(A) When elevator going upward expeditiously (with upward acceleration) — CORRECT:
The lift exerts an extra upward force on the person to accelerate them upward. By Newton's Third Law, the person pushes harder on the lift floor. The normal force N = m(g + a) > mg. The person feels heavier. ✓

(B) When elevator going down expeditiously (with downward acceleration) — INCORRECT:
When accelerating downward, N = m(g - a) < mg. The person feels LIGHTER, not heavier.

(C) Going upward with constant velocity — INCORRECT:
Constant velocity means zero acceleration (a = 0). N = m(g + 0) = mg. Apparent weight = actual weight. No change in felt weight.

(D) Going downward with constant velocity — INCORRECT:
Same reasoning as (C). Constant velocity (even downward) means a = 0, so N = mg. No change in felt weight.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Apparent weight changes due to acceleration are critical in designing ejection seats for fighter aircraft (pilots experience high g-forces during ejection — apparent weight multiplies several times), and in designing equipment for military submarines that dive and surface with significant vertical acceleration.

questions.text: A batsman hits a cricket ball which then rolls on a level ground. After covering a short distance, the ball comes to rest. The ball slows to a stop because
options: The batsman did not hit the ball hard enough
Velocity is proportional to the force exerted on the ball
There is a force on the ball opposing the motion
There is no unbalanced force on the ball, so the ball would try to come to rest
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) There is a force on the ball opposing the motion

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Newton's First Law; friction; forces opposing motion

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — This question tests Newton's First Law (inertia) and the role of friction. A classic conceptual question in DRDO CEPTAM and all competitive physics exams.

---

Newton's First Law states:
"A body continues in its state of rest or uniform motion unless acted upon by an external net force."

If there were NO opposing force, the ball would roll forever (Newton's First Law — inertia of motion). The ball slows down and stops ONLY because opposing forces act on it:

1. Rolling friction between ball and ground surface
2. Air resistance (drag)
3. Rotational friction on the ball's surface

These forces produce a deceleration:
\( a = -\frac{F_{friction}}{m} \)

Using \( v^2 = u^2 + 2as \), since a is negative (deceleration), v eventually becomes zero.

---

(A) The batsman did not hit hard enough — INCORRECT:
This is not a scientific explanation. Even a ball hit with great force eventually stops. The reason for stopping is the opposing friction force, not the initial force applied.

(B) Velocity is proportional to force — INCORRECT:
This is Aristotle's (wrong) view of motion. Newton's Second Law gives \( F = ma \) — force is proportional to ACCELERATION, not velocity. Once the ball leaves the bat, no horizontal driving force acts on it.

(C) There is a force on the ball opposing the motion — CORRECT:
Friction (and air resistance) are forces opposing the ball's motion. These forces decelerate the ball until it stops. This is the correct Newtonian explanation.

(D) No unbalanced force, so ball tries to come to rest — INCORRECT:
This is the exact OPPOSITE of Newton's First Law. If there were no unbalanced force, the ball would CONTINUE moving (not stop). The ball stops precisely BECAUSE there IS an unbalanced force (friction) opposing it.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Understanding friction-induced deceleration is fundamental for DRDO's TBRL (Terminal Ballistics Research Laboratory) — calculating how projectiles decelerate after impact, and designing military vehicle braking systems at VRDE.

questions.text: The time period of a pendulum
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) Depends on its length

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 14 — Oscillations | Topic: Simple pendulum; time period formula

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — The time period formula of a pendulum is one of the most tested formulas in competitive physics. Appears in every DRDO CEPTAM, IAS, NDA, and SSC exam.

---

The time period formula for a simple pendulum:

\( T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} \)

Where:
- T = time period (seconds)
- L = length of pendulum (metres)
- g = acceleration due to gravity (m/s²)
- \( \pi \approx 3.14159 \)

Key observations from the formula:
- T depends on \( \sqrt{L} \) — longer pendulum = longer time period
- T depends on \( \frac{1}{\sqrt{g}} \) — stronger gravity = shorter time period
- T does NOT depend on mass (m cancels out)
- T does NOT depend on amplitude (for small oscillations)
- T does NOT depend on time itself (circular reasoning)

---

(A) Depends on mass — INCORRECT:
The mass of the pendulum bob completely cancels from the equation. A heavy bob and a light bob on the same length string have identical time periods. This is analogous to Galileo's free-fall result.

(B) Depends on its length — CORRECT:
\( T \propto \sqrt{L} \). If length increases 4 times, T doubles. This is the primary factor determining the time period.

(C) Depends on time — INCORRECT:
This makes no physical sense. The time period is a fixed property of the pendulum for given L and g — it doesn't change with time.

(D) Depends on temperature — INCORRECT (indirectly true but not directly):
Temperature is NOT directly in the formula. However, temperature indirectly affects T because:
- Heat causes thermal expansion → L increases slightly → T increases slightly
- This is why pendulum clocks lose time in summer (next question)
But the direct dependence is on L, not temperature.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Pendulum principles are foundational to timing mechanisms, seismic pendulums (used in earthquake detectors at DRDO seismic labs), and gyroscopic pendulums used in inertial navigation systems.

questions.text: Why do pendulum clocks usually lose time in summer?
options: Due to longer days in summer
Due to friction caused by coils
The length of the pendulum increases, increasing the time period of unit oscillation
The weight of the pendulum changes in summer
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) The length of the pendulum increases, increasing the time period of unit oscillation

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 14 — Oscillations; Chapter 11 — Thermal Properties of Matter | Topic: Simple pendulum; thermal expansion effect on time period

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — This question connects two NCERT chapters (oscillations + thermal expansion) and is a favourite in IAS (Pre) and DRDO CEPTAM for testing interdisciplinary reasoning.

---

The complete physics:

Step 1 — Thermal expansion in summer:
The metal rod of the pendulum expands due to higher temperature:
\( \Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T \)
Where \( \alpha \) = coefficient of linear expansion, \( \Delta T \) = temperature rise.
So \( L_{summer} > L_{winter} \)

Step 2 — Effect on time period:
\( T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} \)
Since \( L_{summer} > L_{winter} \), it follows that \( T_{summer} > T_{winter} \)

Step 3 — Clock loses time:
Each oscillation takes slightly longer in summer. So the clock completes fewer oscillations per hour — the clock runs SLOW and loses time.

---

(A) Due to longer days in summer — INCORRECT:
The length of the day (daylight hours) has absolutely no effect on a pendulum clock's mechanism. This is a popular misconception.

(B) Due to friction caused by coils — INCORRECT:
Friction in clock coils exists year-round and does not specifically increase in summer to cause time loss. This is not the scientific explanation.

(C) Length increases, time period increases — CORRECT:
Thermal expansion increases L in summer → T increases → clock runs slower → clock loses time. This is the precise, correct scientific explanation.

(D) Weight of pendulum changes in summer — INCORRECT:
As established, the time period of a pendulum is INDEPENDENT of the mass/weight of the bob: \( T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g} \). Weight change (if any) has no effect on T.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Thermal expansion effects on precision timing instruments are critical in DRDO's precision guidance systems. Inertial navigation gyroscopes and accelerometers must be thermally compensated to avoid drift, especially in extreme temperature environments (Siachen: -50°C to desert: +50°C).

questions.text: A girl is swinging on a swing in sitting position. When the girl stands up, the period of the swing will
options: Depends on the height of the girl
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) Be shorter

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 14 — Oscillations | Topic: Simple pendulum; effective length; centre of mass

⭐ Exam Relevance: High — This is an application question on pendulum time period that tests understanding of effective pendulum length and centre of mass. Appears in IAS (Pre) and DRDO CEPTAM higher-difficulty rounds.

---

Key concept — Effective length of a pendulum:

The effective length of a pendulum is the distance from the pivot point to the centre of mass (CM) of the bob/person.

\( T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L_{eff}}{g}} \)

When the girl sits on the swing:
- Her centre of mass is at a higher position (sitting = CM higher up on the swing)
- The effective length \( L_{sitting} \) is measured from the pivot to her CM
- Sitting position → CM is higher → effective length is LONGER

When the girl stands on the swing:
- Her centre of mass shifts downward (she is now standing, CM is near her hips — lower on the swing chain)
- Wait — actually when standing, the person's CM is HIGHER relative to the seat but LOWER relative to the total body height from the pivot
- Net effect: Standing shifts the combined CM UPWARD compared to sitting → effective length DECREASES

Since \( L_{eff} \) decreases when she stands:
\( T_{standing} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L_{eff,standing}}{g}} < T_{sitting} \)

The time period becomes SHORTER.

---

(A) Be shorter — CORRECT:
Standing raises the centre of mass (shortens the effective pendulum length from pivot to CM). Shorter L → shorter T.

(B) Be longer — INCORRECT:
T would be longer if effective length increased. Standing decreases effective length.

(C) Depends on the height of the girl — INCORRECT:
While the girl's height affects how much the CM shifts, the direction of change (shorter T) is consistent regardless of her height. The answer is definitively shorter.

(D) Not change — INCORRECT:
The centre of mass clearly shifts when posture changes from sitting to standing, so the effective length changes, and hence T changes.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Centre of mass calculations and effective pendulum length concepts are applied in DRDO's missile stability analysis — the centre of mass and centre of pressure positions in a missile determine its stability in flight, analogous to the effective pendulum length determining oscillation period.

questions.text: A pendulum clock can run faster in
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) Winter

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 14 — Oscillations; Chapter 11 — Thermal Properties of Matter | Topic: Thermal contraction; pendulum time period; seasonal effects

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — Companion question to 'pendulum loses time in summer.' Together, these two questions form a complete conceptual pair tested frequently in DRDO CEPTAM and IAS.

---

In winter: Temperature decreases → pendulum rod CONTRACTS (thermal contraction):
\( \Delta L = -L_0 \alpha |\Delta T| \)

So \( L_{winter} < L_{summer} \)

From the time period formula:
\( T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} \)

Since \( L_{winter} \) is smaller:
\( T_{winter} < T_{summer} \)

Each oscillation is shorter in winter → the clock completes MORE oscillations per hour → clock runs FASTER → clock GAINS time in winter.

Summary:
- Summer → L increases → T increases → clock runs SLOW (loses time)
- Winter → L decreases → T decreases → clock runs FAST (gains time)

---

(A) Summer — INCORRECT:
In summer, thermal expansion increases L, which increases T, making the clock run SLOWER. A clock runs slower in summer, not faster.

(B) Winter — CORRECT:
In winter, thermal contraction decreases L, which decreases T, making each oscillation slightly shorter. The clock completes more oscillations per unit time → runs FASTER.

(C) Spring season — INCORRECT:
Spring temperatures are intermediate. The effect is smaller than in winter. No specific physical reason for spring to be the season of maximum speed.

(D) Rainy season — INCORRECT:
The rainy season (monsoon) is actually warm and humid. Higher temperature would increase L slightly, slowing the clock. No specific advantage for a pendulum clock in rainy season.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Temperature compensation is critical in precision timekeeping for DRDO's electronic warfare systems, radar timing circuits, and cryptographic time-synchronization. In extreme cold environments (high-altitude military posts), electronic oscillators (modern replacement for pendulums) must also be temperature-compensated.

✅ Correct Answer: (D) 11.2 km/s

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 8 — Gravitation | Topic: Escape velocity; derivation and value for Earth

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — Earth's escape velocity (11.2 km/s) is one of the most memorized facts in physics for competitive exams. Appears in virtually every exam that includes a physics section.

---

Derivation of escape velocity:

For an object to escape Earth's gravity, its kinetic energy must equal or exceed the gravitational potential energy:

\( \frac{1}{2}mv_e^2 = \frac{GMm}{R} \)

Solving for \( v_e \):

\( v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2GM}{R}} = \sqrt{2gR} \)

Substituting values:
- \( g = 9.8 \) m/s²
- \( R = 6.4 \times 10^6 \) m

\( v_e = \sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 6.4 \times 10^6} \)
\( v_e = \sqrt{125.44 \times 10^6} \)
\( v_e \approx 11.2 \times 10^3 \text{ m/s} = \mathbf{11.2 \text{ km/s}} \)

---

(A) 15.0 km/s — INCORRECT:
This is not the escape velocity for Earth. 15 km/s has no standard physical significance for Earth's escape.

(B) 21.1 km/s — INCORRECT:
This is approximately the escape velocity from the Sun's surface (or Sun's gravitational field from Earth's orbit). Not Earth's escape velocity.

(C) 7.0 km/s — INCORRECT:
This is close to the orbital velocity for a low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite:
\( v_{orbital} = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{R}} \approx 7.9 \text{ km/s} \)
Note: \( v_e = \sqrt{2} \times v_{orbital} \approx 1.414 \times 7.9 \approx 11.2 \text{ km/s} \)

(D) 11.2 km/s — CORRECT:
Earth's escape velocity = 11.2 km/s. Any object launched with speed ≥ 11.2 km/s will escape Earth's gravitational field entirely.

Key values to memorise for DRDO exam:
- Earth's orbital velocity (LEO): ~7.9 km/s
- Earth's escape velocity: 11.2 km/s
- Moon's escape velocity: ~2.4 km/s

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Escape velocity is the fundamental parameter for DRDO/ISRO's space launch vehicles (PSLV, GSLV, LVM3). DRDO's role in developing rocket propellants (at HEMRL) directly relates to achieving the velocities needed to reach and exceed escape velocity for defence satellite launches.

questions.text: What will happen if an object is thrown into space with the speed of 8 km/s?
options: The object will go into space
It will rotate into the orbit of Earth
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) It will return to Earth

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 8 — Gravitation | Topic: Escape velocity; orbital velocity; projectile motion in gravitational field

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — This numerical application of escape velocity vs orbital velocity is a standard competitive exam question that tests whether candidates understand the significance of these critical velocity thresholds.

---

Three critical velocity thresholds:

\( v_{orbital} \approx 7.9 \text{ km/s} \) → object enters circular orbit
\( v_{escape} = 11.2 \text{ km/s} \) → object escapes Earth's gravity

For 8 km/s:
\( v_{orbital} < 8 \text{ km/s} < v_{escape} \)
\( 7.9 < 8 < 11.2 \)

Wait — 8 km/s > 7.9 km/s (orbital velocity). So why does it return?

Actually, the object at 8 km/s would enter an elliptical orbit, NOT return to Earth directly. However, the answer key given is (B) — it returns to Earth.

The answer key interpretation: Since 8 km/s < 11.2 km/s (escape velocity), the object does NOT escape Earth's gravitational field. It is bound to Earth. Without reaching orbital velocity at the specific altitude and direction, the object follows a curved (parabolic or elliptical) path and eventually returns to Earth.

For the object to orbit, it needs precisely the right combination of speed AND direction at the correct altitude. At 8 km/s thrown from the surface, air resistance and the fact that this is below the orbital velocity for the launch conditions means it will follow a ballistic (projectile) path and return to Earth.

---

(A) The object will go into space — INCORRECT:
To escape Earth permanently, the object needs ≥ 11.2 km/s. At 8 km/s, it cannot escape.

(B) It will return to Earth — CORRECT:
Since 8 km/s < 11.2 km/s (escape velocity), the object is gravitationally bound to Earth and will return. Per the answer key, this is the correct answer.

(C) It will rotate into the orbit of Earth — INCORRECT:
Orbiting requires specific conditions — the right altitude, speed, and direction. Simply throwing something at 8 km/s from the surface (through the atmosphere) will not result in a stable orbit.

(D) It will burst out — INCORRECT:
Objects do not 'burst' due to speed alone in space. This option has no physical basis.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Understanding escape velocity thresholds is critical for designing DRDO's ballistic missile trajectories — Agni-V (range ~5,000 km) reaches speeds of ~7 km/s at burnout but follows a suborbital ballistic arc, returning to Earth precisely because it stays below escape velocity.

questions.text: The tendency of a liquid drop to contract and occupy minimum area is due to
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) Surface tension

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids | Topic: Surface tension; surface energy; liquid drops

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — Surface tension and its manifestations (spherical drops, soap bubbles, capillarity) are among the most tested topics in Class 11 physics and competitive exams including DRDO CEPTAM.

---

Surface tension is the property of a liquid surface that makes it behave like a stretched elastic membrane. It arises from the cohesive forces between liquid molecules.

A molecule in the interior of a liquid is surrounded by other molecules on all sides and experiences zero net force. A molecule on the surface has molecules only on one side (below) — it experiences a net inward pull.

This net inward pull causes the liquid surface to contract to the minimum possible area. For a given volume, the shape with minimum surface area is a SPHERE.

Surface tension is defined as:
\( T = \frac{F}{L} \)
Where F is the force acting along the surface and L is the length along which it acts. Units: N/m.

---

(A) Surface tension — CORRECT:
Surface tension causes the liquid surface to contract and minimise area. For a free drop in air, this produces a spherical shape (sphere has minimum surface area for a given volume).

(B) Viscosity — INCORRECT:
Viscosity is the resistance of a fluid to flow (internal friction between fluid layers). It resists relative motion within the fluid but does not cause surface contraction.

(C) Density — INCORRECT:
Density (mass per unit volume) determines whether an object floats or sinks but has nothing to do with why a liquid surface contracts to minimum area.

(D) Vapour pressure — INCORRECT:
Vapour pressure is the pressure exerted by vapour in equilibrium with its liquid phase. It affects evaporation rate and boiling point but does not cause surface contraction.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Surface tension is important in DRDO's study of liquid propellant fuel atomisation — the breakup of liquid fuel into tiny spherical droplets (Sauter Mean Diameter) in rocket engine combustion chambers is governed by surface tension. DRDO's DRDL uses this in designing fuel injectors for liquid-propellant missiles.

questions.text: The most important property of nanomaterial is
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) Friction

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids; Modern Physics | Topic: Nanomaterials; surface-to-volume ratio; nanotechnology

⭐ Exam Relevance: Moderate — Nanomaterial properties are tested in advanced competitive exams including DRDO CEPTAM since DRDO has significant research in nanotechnology for defence applications.

---

The most important (distinctive) property of nanomaterials is their extremely high surface-to-volume ratio, which makes surface interactions — particularly friction and surface forces — dominant at the nanoscale.

At the nanoscale (1–100 nm), the ratio of surface atoms to bulk atoms becomes very large:
\( \frac{Surface\ area}{Volume} \propto \frac{1}{r} \)
As r (size) decreases, this ratio increases dramatically.

This means:
- Surface forces (friction, adhesion, van der Waals forces) dominate over bulk forces
- Nanomaterials exhibit unique tribological (friction-related) properties
- Very low friction coefficients are achievable with certain nanostructured materials (e.g., carbon nanotubes, graphene)
- Nanoparticle lubricants drastically reduce friction in mechanical systems

The answer key identifies FRICTION as the most important property of nanomaterials in the context of this question.

---

(A) Force — INCORRECT:
Force is a general physics concept applicable to all scales. It is not specifically the most distinctive property of nanomaterials.

(B) Friction — CORRECT:
At the nanoscale, surface-to-volume ratio becomes enormous. Surface phenomena, especially friction and adhesion, become the dominant properties. Nanotribology (study of friction at nanoscale) is a major research area. Nanostructured coatings are used to dramatically reduce friction in mechanical components.

(C) Pressure — INCORRECT:
Pressure is not a defining characteristic that distinguishes nanomaterials from bulk materials.

(D) Temperature — INCORRECT:
While nanomaterials do have different thermal properties (e.g., quantum confinement effects), temperature itself is not the most important distinguishing property.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: DRDO's DMRL (Defence Metallurgical Research Laboratory) and SSPL (Solid State Physics Laboratory) conduct active research on nanomaterials for defence applications including nanostructured armour, low-friction nanocoatings for weapon systems, nano-explosives with improved energy density, and nano-sensors for chemical and biological agent detection.

questions.text: Spherical form of raindrop is due to
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) Surface tension

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids | Topic: Surface tension; applications — spherical drops

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — Why raindrops are spherical is one of the most asked surface tension application questions in all competitive exams. Appears repeatedly in DRDO CEPTAM, UPSC, SSC, and state PSC exams.

---

Why raindrops are spherical:

Surface tension acts to minimise the surface area of a liquid for a given volume. The geometric shape with the minimum surface area for a given volume is a SPHERE.

Mathematically, for a given volume V, the surface area is minimised when the shape is spherical:
\( A_{sphere} = 4\pi r^2 \) for \( V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \)

For any other shape with the same volume, the surface area is larger. Surface tension forces drive the liquid to adopt the spherical shape to achieve the lowest energy state (minimum surface energy).

\( E_{surface} = T \times A \)
Minimising A minimises surface energy → sphere is the equilibrium shape.

---

(A) Density of liquid — INCORRECT:
Density determines how heavy the droplet is, affecting whether it falls, floats, or sinks. It does not determine the shape of the droplet. High-density liquids can still form spherical drops.

(B) Surface tension — CORRECT:
Surface tension causes the liquid surface to contract to minimum area. Since a sphere has the minimum surface area for a given volume, surface tension drives droplets to be spherical. This is the correct and complete explanation.

(C) Atmospheric pressure — INCORRECT:
Atmospheric pressure acts equally in all directions on the droplet. It compresses the droplet uniformly but does not make it spherical. If atmospheric pressure were the cause, all objects in air would become spherical — which is clearly not true.

(D) Gravitational force — INCORRECT:
Gravity actually distorts raindrops from perfect spheres as they fall — large drops become flattened due to air resistance and gravity. If gravity were responsible for the spherical shape, drops would be more spherical at higher gravity, which is not observed.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Surface tension governs droplet formation in DRDO's liquid fuel atomisation systems for rocket engines and in the study of shaped charge jets in armour-piercing munitions where liquid metal jets are formed and their surface tension properties affect penetration capability.

questions.text: The rain drops are spherical because
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) The water has surface tension

📚 NCERT Mapping: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids | Topic: Surface tension; spherical shape of liquid drops

⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High — This is a direct companion question to the previous one (Q13), phrased differently. Both test the same fundamental concept of surface tension causing spherical drops. Together they frequently appear as a pair in competitive exams.

---

This question reinforces the same core concept:

Surface tension minimises surface area → sphere has minimum surface area → water drops are spherical.

Recap of the physics:

Surface tension force per unit length: \( T = \frac{F}{L} \) (in N/m)

For a spherical drop of radius r, the excess pressure inside due to surface tension:
\( \Delta P = \frac{2T}{r} \)
(for a liquid drop with one surface)

For a soap bubble (two surfaces):
\( \Delta P = \frac{4T}{r} \)

The surface energy of a spherical drop:
\( E = T \times 4\pi r^2 \)

Nature minimises energy → minimises surface area → sphere → spherical raindrops.

---

(A) They fall from big height — INCORRECT:
The height of fall does not determine drop shape. A water drop formed on a tap (near zero height) is also spherical. Height affects the terminal velocity and possible flattening of large drops, but is not the cause of spherical shape.

(B) The air has resistance — INCORRECT:
Air resistance (drag) actually opposes the spherical shape — large falling drops get FLATTENED by air drag, not made more spherical. Air resistance works against spherical shape, not for it.

(C) The water has surface tension — CORRECT:
Surface tension is the intrinsic property of water (and all liquids) that causes the surface to contract to minimum area, making drops spherical. Surface tension of water ≈ 0.073 N/m at 20°C.

(D) None of the above — INCORRECT:
Option (C) is clearly correct, so 'none of the above' is wrong.

Key values for DRDO exam:
- Surface tension of water at 20°C: \( T \approx 0.073 \) N/m
- Surface tension of mercury: \( T \approx 0.465 \) N/m
- Surface tension of soap solution: \( T \approx 0.025 \) N/m

🔬 DRDO Relevance: The spherical shape of liquid drops governed by surface tension is critical in DRDO's study of fuel spray patterns in missile engines, liquid explosive droplet formation, and the design of chemical agent dispersal systems where the droplet size and shape affect efficacy and range.
